Understanding Nuclear Dynamics

Holy
Nuclear
Warfare

The power that reshaped humanity — explore the history, science, and consequences of the most destructive force ever created.

↓ scroll to explore

01 — What Are Nuclear Weapons

Energy that
moves worlds.
And ends them.

Nuclear weapons release immense energy from atomic reactions — either splitting atoms apart (fission) or fusing them together (fusion). The result is an explosion capable of levelling entire cities in seconds. But beyond raw destruction, nuclear weapons are defined by what they represent: the moment humanity gained the power to end itself.

01.2 — The Science

How Fission Becomes
Devastation.

A single slow neutron strikes a uranium-235 nucleus. The nucleus absorbs it, becomes unstable, and splits — releasing a burst of energy and 2–3 new neutrons. Each of those neutrons strikes another nucleus. In less than a microsecond this exponential cascade — the chain reaction — releases the energy equivalent of thousands of tons of TNT.

Click anywhere on the diagram to fire the first neutron and watch it unfold.

0 / 0 nuclei fissioned

↑ Click to fire neutron

01
Neutron absorption

A free neutron enters a U-235 nucleus. The nucleus absorbs it and becomes U-236 — an unstable, excited state.

02
Nuclear fission

U-236 splits into two smaller nuclei (krypton + barium), releasing ~200 MeV of energy — mostly as kinetic energy and gamma rays.

03
Neutron release

Each fission releases 2–3 free neutrons. These travel outward and are now available to strike more nuclei, multiplying the reaction.

04
Exponential cascade

1 → 3 → 9 → 27 → 81… In 80 generations (under 1 microsecond), a supercritical mass undergoes a full nuclear explosion.

01.5 — Anatomy

Inside the Bomb.

A technical cross-section of "Fat Man" — the plutonium implosion device dropped on Nagasaki, August 9, 1945. Hover components to reveal their function.

Schematic is illustrative and based on declassified public sources. Not to scale.

02 — History & Evolution

The Arms Race

1939
The Beginning

1939

Einstein Writes
to Roosevelt

Albert Einstein and Leo Szilard warn President Roosevelt that Nazi Germany may be developing atomic weapons. This letter sets in motion the Manhattan Project — the most ambitious scientific undertaking in history, involving over 130,000 people across three countries.

July 16, 1945

Trinity.
The First Detonation.

In the New Mexico desert, the world's first nuclear device detonates. The explosion equals 21,000 tons of TNT. J. Robert Oppenheimer recalls a line from Hindu scripture: "Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds." Humanity has crossed a threshold it can never uncross.

August 6, 1945

Hiroshima
Erased.

At 8:15 AM, "Little Boy" detonates 600 metres above Hiroshima. 70,000 people die instantly. By the end of 1945, the death toll reaches 140,000. Shadows of vaporised human beings are burned into stone steps. Nothing in the history of warfare had ever moved this fast.

August 9, 1945

Nagasaki.
Three Days Later.

At 11:02 AM, "Fat Man" — a plutonium implosion bomb — detonates 500 metres above Nagasaki. 40,000 die instantly; the toll reaches 80,000 by year's end. Japan surrenders six days later. These remain the only two nuclear weapons ever used against human beings — a record humanity cannot afford to break.

1949

The Soviets
Test Joe-1.

The United States loses its nuclear monopoly. The Soviet Union detonates its first atomic bomb, shocking Western intelligence who thought it would take until 1953. The Cold War arms race begins in earnest. Two superpowers now hold the power to destroy each other — and the world.

March 1, 1954

Castle Bravo.
The Fallout Spreads.

The largest US nuclear test — 15 megatons, 1,000 times Hiroshima — contaminates 7,000 square miles of Pacific Ocean. The crew of the Japanese fishing vessel Daigo Fukuryū Maru (Lucky Dragon No. 5) are exposed to radioactive fallout 80 miles from the detonation. All 23 crew members develop acute radiation sickness. The incident shocks the world and galvanises the global anti-nuclear movement.

1962

13 Days
From Annihilation.

Soviet missiles discovered in Cuba. For 13 days, the world holds its breath. A single miscommunication, a single order misread — and the missiles fly. The crisis ends, but reveals how precarious the balance truly is. We came closer to nuclear war than most people know.

1947–1991

The Cold War
Arms Race.

The US and Soviet Union engaged in a decades-long nuclear build-up — "if my enemy has nuclear weapons, I should too." By 1986 the combined global arsenal peaked at over 70,000 warheads. Gorbachev's glasnost and perestroika opened a path forward: at Reykjavik in 1986 he proposed a 50% reduction, and in 1987 both nations signed the INF Treaty, eliminating an entire class of nuclear weapons. Mutually Assured Destruction had held — but at terrifying cost.

Today

12,500
Warheads Remain.

Nine countries now possess nuclear weapons. Despite arms reduction treaties, the global arsenal retains the capacity for civilisation-ending destruction many times over. The weapons have never been used since Nagasaki — but they have never stopped existing either.

By the numbers.

01 / 04 — Applications

Mutually
Assured
Destruction

MAD is the doctrine that kept the Cold War cold. If both sides can annihilate each other, neither dares strike first. It is deterrence through existential terror — a balance of power maintained by the shared guarantee of total destruction. Grotesque in theory. Effective in practice.

02 / 04 — Applications

Nuclear
Power
Generation

The same fission reaction that destroyed Hiroshima powers millions of homes. Nuclear power produces enormous energy with near-zero carbon emissions. Today it supplies 10% of the world's electricity. The debate over its safety versus its necessity defines the energy conversation of our century.

03 / 04 — Applications

Medical
Applications

Radiation that kills in war saves lives in medicine. Radiotherapy targets and destroys cancer cells. Nuclear imaging — PET scans, MRI — allows doctors to see inside the human body with extraordinary precision. The same physics that powers bombs has become one of medicine's most powerful tools.

04 / 04 — Applications

Strategic
Deterrence
Today

Modern nuclear strategy goes far beyond MAD. Submarine-launched missiles ensure second-strike capability. Tactical nukes blur the line between conventional and nuclear war. As new powers join the nuclear club and old treaties collapse, the strategic landscape grows more complex — and more dangerous — by the decade.

04 — Implications

The Cost
of Power.

01
Humanitarian Cost

The two bombs dropped on Japan killed between 129,000 and 226,000 people — mostly civilians. Survivors, known as hibakusha, faced radiation sickness, cancers, and social stigma for decades. The humanitarian case against nuclear weapons is built on these lives.

02
Environmental Impact

A large-scale nuclear exchange could trigger "nuclear winter" — soot and debris blocking sunlight for years, causing global crop failure and mass starvation. Even a limited regional nuclear war between India and Pakistan could kill more people through famine than through the blasts themselves.

03
Geopolitical Tension

Nuclear weapons do not just kill — they reshape international order. They grant their possessors a form of immunity from conventional military pressure. Nations that acquire them change their standing in global politics permanently. Proliferation is thus not merely a security issue but a civilisational one.

04 — Implications in Today's Conflict Regional Map Nuclear Risk Energy Escalation

Nuclear Warfare in
Today's Conflict Landscape

The recent Middle East war shows how quickly conventional conflict can escalate into regional instability, infrastructure risk, humanitarian collapse, and fears of nuclear confrontation.

01 — Regional instability

A conflict that refuses to stay contained

What begins as a conflict between a few states can quickly expand across borders through proxy groups, retaliation, and military alliances. Yemen's Houthis entered the war by launching attacks on Israel, while U.S. forces increased their presence in the region.

Once more actors join the conflict, it becomes harder to contain, predict, or de-escalate. What starts as a conventional war can rapidly become a broader regional security crisis.

"What starts as a conventional war can rapidly become a broader regional security crisis — involving actors no one initially anticipated."

① Yemen → Israel

The Houthi movement in Yemen has been firing drones and ballistic missiles at Israel since Oct 2023, declaring solidarity with Palestinians. Yemen is attacking Israel.

② Iran → Yemen & Lebanon

Iran funds, arms, and directs the Houthis (Yemen) and Hezbollah (Lebanon). Iran is Israel's enemy — these groups are Iran's proxies used to fight Israel without Iran attacking directly.

③ U.S. in the Gulf

The U.S. deployed carrier strike groups to the Persian Gulf. They have intercepted Houthi missiles and struck Houthi targets in Yemen. The U.S. is backing Israel.

— Yemen (Houthis) strikes Israel — Iran funds/arms proxy forces — U.S. intercepts & presence Hover countries for details · 2023–2026
Role in conflict

Nuclear relevance

02 — Infrastructure & nuclear-site risk

Strikes near nuclear infrastructure

In modern war, the danger is not only nuclear weapons themselves, but also attacks near nuclear-related infrastructure. The current conflict has raised alarm over strikes near Iran's Bushehr nuclear power plant.

Even without a nuclear detonation, military action near nuclear facilities creates fears of radiation release, contamination, panic, and international escalation. Conventional war can generate nuclear-related consequences simply by moving too close.

"Military action near nuclear facilities creates fears of radiation release — blurring the line between conventional and nuclear consequence."

03 — Energy & trade disruption

When oil routes become conflict casualties

A regional war in the Middle East does not stay regional when oil routes and shipping corridors are threatened. Reuters reported that the conflict disrupted energy supplies through the Strait of Hormuz, while Houthi involvement raised concern around major shipping routes.

Gulf markets reacted to fears of broader conflict, and Brent crude rose sharply to above US$112 per barrel. Modern conflict can trigger global economic consequences even before the fighting spreads further.

Strait of Hormuz
~20% of global oil transits this chokepoint daily

04 — Nuclear escalation

From conventional war to nuclear threshold

The danger is not only an actual launch. It is the growing possibility of miscalculation, escalation, and weaker global safeguards. On 5 February 2026, the UN Secretary-General described the expiry of New START as a grave moment for international peace and security.

Every regional crisis involving nuclear-capable states or nuclear infrastructure feels more dangerous — because there are fewer guardrails left.

"In modern warfare, the greatest danger may not be a nuclear launch alone, but how quickly conflict can move the world closer to one."

05

Miscalculation risk becomes critical
Nuclear sites enter the conflict zone. Military operations near Bushehr raise alarms beyond conventional rules of engagement. Even without nuclear weapons being used, proximity creates radiation risk, panic, and international alarm — blurring the line between conventional and nuclear consequence.
Conclusion

In modern warfare, the greatest danger may not be a nuclear launch alone, but how quickly conflict can move the world closer to one.

New START expired 5 February 2026 — UN Secretary-General: "a grave moment for international peace and security."

04 — Prevention & Treaties

The Guardrails
We Built.

Beyond Mutually Assured Destruction, humanity has built a fragile but real architecture of international law to constrain nuclear weapons. These treaties represent the collective attempt to step back from the brink — some have held, some have collapsed.

NPT
Non-Proliferation Treaty

The cornerstone of the global nuclear order since 1970. 191 states have signed, agreeing not to acquire nuclear weapons in exchange for civilian nuclear access and a disarmament commitment from existing nuclear states. North Korea withdrew in 2003. The treaty's disarmament pillar has never been fully honoured.

↗ Read more
TPNW
Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons

Adopted in 2017, entered into force 2021 — the first legally binding international agreement to comprehensively prohibit nuclear weapons. 93 signatories. Notably, all nine nuclear-armed states and all NATO members have refused to join. A moral statement that has yet to gain the signatories needed for real enforcement.

↗ Read more
INF
Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty

Signed in 1987 by Reagan and Gorbachev after Reykjavik, the INF Treaty eliminated an entire class of nuclear weapons — ground-launched missiles with ranges of 500–5,500 km. It was a landmark of Cold War détente. The US withdrew in 2019, citing Russian violations. The weapons it banned are being developed again.

↗ Read more
SEANWFZ
Southeast Asian Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone

The Bangkok Treaty (1997) established Southeast Asia as a nuclear-weapon-free zone. All ten ASEAN states are signatories. The five recognised nuclear powers have yet to sign its Protocol — meaning their nuclear submarines can still transit the zone. It represents a regional model that has inspired similar zones in Africa, Latin America, and the Pacific.

↗ Read more
"The unleashed power of the atom has changed everything save our modes of thinking, and we thus drift toward unparalleled catastrophe."

— Albert Einstein, 1946

07 — The Future

World War
IV?

Einstein once quipped that he did not know with what weapons World War III would be fought — but World War IV would be fought with sticks and stones. The implication was clear: a nuclear third world war would erase the civilisation capable of fighting a fourth. That logic has held for eighty years. The question now is whether it will hold for eighty more.

01
Proliferation Risk

Nine states now possess nuclear weapons. Iran stands at the threshold. The NPT framework is eroding — the US withdrew from the INF Treaty in 2019, and Russia suspended New START in 2023. Every new nuclear state lowers the threshold for first use, and increases the probability of miscalculation. The Bulletin of Atomic Scientists moved the Doomsday Clock to 89 seconds to midnight in 2024 — the closest it has ever been.

02
Great Power Competition

The US-China rivalry over Taiwan represents the most plausible flashpoint for great-power conflict in the 21st century. China is projected to possess 1,500 warheads by 2035. Russia's doctrine explicitly permits nuclear first use if conventional defeat is imminent — a doctrine stress-tested in Ukraine since 2022. The three-way nuclear competition between the US, Russia, and China has no Cold War precedent and no established guardrails.

03
AI & Autonomous Systems

Artificial intelligence is being integrated into nuclear command-and-control systems at a pace that outstrips our understanding of its failure modes. A 2023 RAND Corporation study found that AI-enabled early warning systems could compress decision timelines from minutes to seconds — eliminating the human reflection that prevented nuclear war during the 1983 Stanislav Petrov incident. Autonomous weapons that can escalate without human authorisation may be the defining risk of the coming decade.

04
Climate & Resource Conflict

Climate change is a conflict multiplier. By 2050, water scarcity will affect over 5 billion people. The India-Pakistan nuclear dyad sits atop one of the world's most climate-stressed regions — the Himalayan glaciers that feed both nations' rivers are retreating at record pace. A 2022 study in Nature Food found that even a limited India-Pakistan nuclear exchange could trigger a global famine killing up to 2 billion people, entirely through agricultural disruption.

The Stakes

A nuclear war has
no winners.

The Doomsday Clock

How Close Are We?

89
seconds to midnight
2024 — Closest ever

What is the Doomsday Clock? It's a symbolic clock created in 1947 by the Bulletin of Atomic Scientists — a group of researchers who built the first atomic bomb. The clock face represents the full 60-minute journey from "normal times" to total catastrophe. Midnight = global nuclear annihilation. Every year, the scientists assess global nuclear risk, climate threats, and disruptive technologies, then move the clock hands accordingly.

Why does it matter? The minute hand tells you, in plain visual terms, how close humanity is to self-destruction. It is not a literal clock — it is expert scientific consensus expressed as a metaphor that anyone can immediately grasp.

How the clock has moved

Risk Assessment — Expert Consensus

How Likely Is It?

The probability estimates below are drawn from surveys of nuclear security experts conducted by the Global Catastrophic Risk Institute and the Future of Life Institute between 2020 and 2024.

Nuclear weapon use by 2045 ~20%
Nuclear exchange between major powers by 2100 ~10%
Nuclear terrorism incident by 2040 ~5%
Civilisation-ending nuclear war this century ~1–3%

The deterrent has held.
For now.

The absence of nuclear war since 1945 is not proof that nuclear deterrence works indefinitely — it may simply be evidence that we have been lucky. The Cuban Missile Crisis came within a single submarine officer's veto of nuclear exchange. In 1983, a Soviet early-warning satellite falsely detected five incoming US missiles; only Stanislav Petrov's instinct to distrust the alert prevented a retaliatory launch. Luck is not a strategy. The question for this generation is whether humanity can build the institutions, the treaties, and the shared understanding needed to ensure that the weapons created in 1945 are never used again — before the next near-miss becomes a hit.
